# Transcrição: 09-Going Deeper_ Emails that Invite

## [00:00 - 03:01]

All right, it is finally time to invite your friends, your email subscribers, to leave Indecision Island for good and to move over to Results Island with you. It's time to send an invitational email. Now, this is step four. When we look at the entire email journey, this is the last step. This is where we make it very clear how those on the island, those getting our email, are how they can make a difference, how they can start to change, and that's with what we're offering them through this email. For your invitational emails, we're going to be focusing on three different stages. Now the first thing that we'll be inviting our email subscribers to is something that's free, a free resource. So this could be a PDF. This could be a workshop. This could be a video to watch. This is the first level of invitational emails. It's simply saying, hey, do you want this? It's free, literally. Free 99. No dollars. All you have to do is click and you can get started. So that's the first kind of invitational email is just simply saying, I made this for you and you can have it. No expectations. Nothing asked of you. Just click right here and get started. These are one of the best kinds of emails to send for invitation because you're going to get the most results because everybody loves free. And so getting the free resource to your subscribers and getting them onto that track, helping them start to get a quick win is so powerful. Now the next kind of invitational email you'll send is to get them to attend some kind of sales mechanism, some kind of sales event. Now don't worry, Dean teaches you this inside the Mastermind program, but this is the moment where you want them to show up. We often talk about how many people are going to show up. So if you're teaching a workshop or a webinar, if you're having some kind of live event, then we want to be sending lots of invitational emails that make sure, one, that they sign up to come and two, that they actually show up. So invitational emails in the second stage are all about attendance. They're all about making sure that you get a moment face to face or screen to screen with this reader, that they're going to take it beyond the email and go to a much more personalized experience. Then our third stage of invitational emails is when we're actually making the sale. This is when we're presenting our product and we're saying, hey, this is available, would you like it? Now this is going to make up such a small part of our invitational emails and you've earned the right to sell because you have taught them. You have shown them. You have entertained them and you've even given them free resources and ways to learn from you over and over and over again. So they're not going to be surprised.

## [03:01 - 06:04]

In fact, they're going to wonder what's wrong if they never get the email that says, hey, here's my product. I think you should buy it. These three invitational emails are so vital to help move your subscribers to the next stage of their journey. To the free resource, the sales mechanism or sales event and then finally the purchase of the product. Now there's something I want you to include in each and every invitational email and that's what I call the US factor. You stands for urgency. This means that there's going to be a time limitation on this invitation. Now we know that's just the nature of an invitation, right? If I get an invite to a party, it doesn't say, hey, this party's happening all day, every day, forever. Feel free to show up when you want to. No, the invitation says this party's happening on this day at this time, RSVP if you want to seat. And that's what we need to have with our emails. With these invitations, they need to have urgency around them. They need to have a time constraint. This is happening within a week. This is happening tomorrow. This is happening in the next hour. We want to have urgency because guess what? We know that we hate making decisions and we also know that our email readers hate making decisions too. So when we add that urgency, it encourages your reader to not just scroll past, to not just close the email, but to go ahead and answer the invitation. The next thing we want to have the S of the US factor is scarcity. Now scarcity is going to show that there aren't a time, that there's not a never ending availability of this particular resource. And we know that to be true about all things in life, right? What is there besides lettuce in my garden that has a never ending supply? I'm just kidding, the lettuce does run out. There's nothing, right? There's nothing that we can pull from and pull from and pull from and take all we want again and again and again, and there's never an end to it. And that's what we need to promote and explain in our invitational emails with the S, the scarcity factor. So you can do this in a lot of ways. You can say we have this many spots. You can say I've reserved this many seats. You can say I have this many time blocks. You could say if it's a resource that you're shipping or sending, you can say we only have this particular number. You can set a wait list and say only a certain percentage of this wait list will get accepted. The U.S. factor is the thing that moves people off of indecision. It is the one necessary component you've got to have in every single invitational email. So whether you're offering a free resource, inviting them to a sales moment, or offering explicitly for them to buy the product, there always needs to be urgency and scarcity in each of those emails. Now, invitational emails don't have to feel like a sales call.

## [06:04 - 09:06]

They don't have to feel like you're knocking on the door and saying, hey, buy my stuff. Buy my stuff. Would you like to buy my stuff? Instead, we're going to bring back the elements that we've learned in the other three stages of our email journey. Every invitational email that you send, I want you to pick one of our three types of messaging to use in that email. For instance, if you're going to be inviting them to download a resource, you could use our informing step. And in the informing email, you're going to pull out from your download, the resource that your readers are going to get for free, pull out a really small section of that download, and just provide it right there in the email. So for me, I have a planting guide that I give as a free resource. And in there, I detail the sizes of plants by category. So what I could do is pull out just one little part, not even all the categories, but just one category. And in my email, I could detail that one thing about that plant category and say, hey, this is just a small part of what's inside my planting guide. Another thing I could do for that same resource is showcase the results. Let's say I had a student who downloaded the guide and then sent me a photo of their garden that is packed full of plants and completely exploding with growth. Well, that email could be a showcase email, but it's also an invitational email because I say, hey, look at this person's results. Want to know how they got it? They got this download and you should too. Finally, I could make it an entertaining email. I could tell a story about that guide that helps people relate to it and like and trust me as I'm offering the guide. This helps them trust that when they click to get that guide, I'm not going to send them something spammy. I could tell them a simple story of how I learned to plant this way by going to national parks. I could open up with that quick story of a little vacation with my family back years ago and how going and seeing all those plants growing in the wild made me create my gardens the way I do now and that I put it all inside the guide. Do you see how we can use steps one of entertainment, steps two of education and steps three of showcasing. We can use those types of messaging even when we're writing a sales email, even when we're writing an email that's asking someone to buy a product from us. When we use this type of messaging, it softens things and makes things so much more enjoyable and the most important is it guides our readers to get to the end of the email because at the end of the email is the CTA that called to action at the end of the email is the final invitation to go to the next step and we need all of our readers to make it to the bottom of that email so that they say yes and they move one step away from indecision and one step closer to results.
